subject: [RFC PATCH 00/10] mm/swap: ghost swapfile with backend switching via Redirect entries

reviews.author: Chris Li
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Chris Li expressed a positive first impression of the patch series, stating it is better than previous versions and incorporating Kairui's feedback, but requested time to review further.

Reviewer Chris Li requested that the Virtual Swap (VS) implementation support the ghost swapfile usage case without increasing metadata overhead compared to the existing ghost swapfile deployment, aiming for an increase of less than 1 byte per swap slot.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Nhat,

Sorry for the late reply. I've been busy and just saw this.  First
impression looks promising, it is better than any previous version of
VS. Thanks for incorporating Kairui's feedback. Please give me some
time to sleep on it. I will get back to you on that.

---

May I add one wishlist item related to ghost swapfile here? I will
reply to your VS thread for other feedback.

For the ghost swapfile usage case (no writeback). Can VS support this
usage case while maintaining per-swap-slot metadata usage comparable
to the ghost swapfile implementation? If we have to put a number to
it, let's say the increase in metadata is less than 1 byte per swap
slot compared to the ghost swapfile implementation.

We have large deployments of ghost swapfile in the fleet. I want an
upstream aligned solution that does not increase the existing memory
overhead compared to the current ghost swapfile deployment, for that
usage. As you know, memory pricing and optimization pressure are high.
The ghost swapfile patch itself is relatively simple. If VS can
address this usage case, it will remove a lot of incentive to explore
ghost swapfile-like solutions.

Thanks

Chris

2026-07-15.patch_summary: This patch series proposes a new design for ghost swapfiles with backend switching via Redirect entries, addressing the issue of writeback support and redesigning around it. The goal is to create a single coherent design that solves the larger problem. The approach involves solving one piece of the puzzle at a time, starting with an optimization patch series and then incorporating features such as dynamic address space allocation and xarray-based backend storage. The design aims to be cleaner and more efficient than existing solutions, while allowing for flexibility in terms of metadata management and swap cache operations.
